senses.definition: to agree to something, such as a suggestion, request, etc.

examples.x: During the meeting, members of the committee were asked to assent to the proposed changes in the project plan.
In a democratic process, citizens cast their votes to assent to or dissent from proposed legislation.
As a form of politeness, participants often assent by nodding or giving a verbal agreement during discussions.
In a democratic process, citizens assented by casting their votes in favor of the proposed legislation.
The board of directors assented to the budget adjustments.

senses.definition: to reluctantly accept something without protest

examples.x: The student, unable to convince the teacher otherwise, acquiesced and accepted the lower grade on the assignment.
The company reluctantly acquiesced to the demands of the striking workers and agreed to negotiate better working conditions.
Despite her reservations, she decided to acquiesce to their demands in order to avoid conflict.
He eventually acquiesced to the terms of the contract, although he wasn't entirely satisfied with them.
The board of directors reluctantly acquiesced to the CEO's decision, even though some members disagreed.

senses.definition: to show one's agreement or approval of an online post or comment by clicking on a specific icon

examples.x: Users are encouraged to upvote content that they find valuable or interesting to increase its visibility.
If you agree with a particular viewpoint, you can upvote the comment to show your support.
The community tends to upvote posts that are informative, well-written, or entertaining.
As a moderator, it's important to upvote contributions that adhere to the community guidelines.
Don't forget to upvote posts that you find helpful or insightful to show appreciation for the effort put into them.

senses.definition: to agree and not oppose to something that one generally finds unacceptable or unpleasant

examples.x: Despite his personal reservations, he decided to countenance the new policy to maintain harmony within the team.
She couldn't countenance the idea of firing her longtime employee, even though his performance had been declining.
The committee was unwilling to countenance any further delays in the project schedule.
The government refused to countenance any form of corruption within its ranks.

senses.definition: to agree to something such as a request, proposal, demand, etc.
to stop resisting something and accept it

examples.x: Faced with overwhelming opposition, the army decided to capitulate and surrender.
The negotiators are capitulating to some of the demands to reach an agreement.
She initially resisted the idea but eventually capitulated when she saw the benefits.
The firm capitulated to the competitive market forces and lowered its prices.
The general decided to capitulate rather than risk further loss of troops.

senses.definition: to accept something, usually after some resistance

examples.x: After much persuasion, the manager relented and granted the team an extra day off.
Despite his initial stubbornness, he eventually relented and agreed to compromise.
The company finally relented and decided to lower their prices in response to customer feedback.
She pleaded with him to relent and forgive her, promising to make amends.
The teacher relented and extended the deadline for the assignment after considering the students' requests.

senses.definition: to show one's disagreement or disapproval of an online post or comment by clicking on a specific icon

examples.x: Users are encouraged to downvote content that violates community guidelines or is deemed low quality.
If you disagree with a particular viewpoint, you can downvote the comment to express your disapproval.
The community tends to downvote posts that are irrelevant, offensive, or contain misinformation.
As a moderator, it's important to downvote contributions that are spammy or inflammatory.
Don't hesitate to downvote posts that you find inappropriate or harmful to discourage similar behavior in the future.

senses.definition: (of views, opinions, etc.) to be different from each other

examples.x: During the debate, the candidates' views began to diverge on key economic policies.
Over time, the political parties' stances started to diverge, leading to increased polarization.
The survey results indicated that public opinions on the matter tended to diverge.
In the family meeting, it was apparent that the siblings' perspectives would diverge regarding the inheritance distribution.
The panel of experts expected their conclusions to diverge due to differing research methodologies.

senses.definition: to give or have opinions that differ from those officially or commonly accepted

examples.x: Many in the community dissented when the city proposed building a new highway through the park.
Students are encouraged to dissent respectfully and engage in constructive debate in the classroom.

senses.definition: to strongly argue, disapprove, or disagree with someone or something

examples.x: Yesterday, I expostulated with my colleague about their unprofessional behavior.
He is currently expostulating with the customer service representative over the poor quality of the product.
Tomorrow, I will expostulate with my landlord about the sudden increase in rent.

senses.definition: to disagree or deny that something is true

examples.x: No one could gainsay the evidence presented by the prosecution; it was indisputable.
Despite her efforts to gainsay the allegations, the truth eventually came to light.
It is difficult to gainsay the impact of climate change on our environment.
His impeccable track record in the industry is hard to gainsay; he is widely respected for his achievements.
The witness's testimony directly gainsayed the defendant's alibi, casting doubt on their innocence.

senses.definition: ‌to express disapproval of something by making a noise in the throat

examples.x: Whenever the topic of politics came up at the family dinner table, Uncle Bob would inevitably harrumph and change the subject.

senses.definition: to not support and be against something or someone

examples.x: The mayor deprecated the use of violence as a means of protest, urging citizens to seek peaceful alternatives.
She deprecated the company's decision to cut employee benefits, arguing that it would harm morale and productivity.
The teacher deprecated cheating in any form and emphasized the importance of academic integrity.
He deprecated the government's failure to address the growing income inequality in the country.
The community leaders deprecated the rise of hate speech and discrimination, calling for unity and tolerance instead.

senses.definition: to disapprove of or have a negative opinion about something, particularly due to being improper or unacceptable

examples.x: In their workplace, any form of socializing beyond work-related matters is frowned on.
Her family tends to frown on her dating someone much older than she is.
While some enjoy it, others frown on the use of profanity in public speeches.

senses.definition: to dismiss or reject something as false

examples.x: The company repudiated the claims that their products were unsafe, presenting evidence to the contrary.
Despite the circulating rumors, he was quick to repudiate any allegations of wrongdoing.
The company repudiated the allegations of unethical business practices, stating that they were unfounded.
She repudiated the rumors circulating about her personal life, insisting that they were baseless.
The government repudiated the claims made by the opposition party, asserting that they were politically motivated.

senses.definition: to intentionally make harmful statements to damage a person or thing's worth or reputation

examples.x: The tabloid newspaper consistently denigrated the celebrity, spreading false rumors to tarnish their reputation.
The political opponent resorted to denigrating the candidate's character rather than focusing on policy differences.
In a heated argument, she denigrated her coworker, making false accusations to harm their professional standing.
The online troll denigrated the author's work with malicious reviews, attempting to damage the book's reputation.
Rather than offering constructive criticism, the critic chose to denigrate the artist, questioning their talent and integrity.

senses.definition: to behave in a way that lowers the dignity or respect of oneself or others

examples.x: He demeaned himself by speaking rudely to the waiter.
The politician was demeaning his opponent in a heated debate.
Stop demeaning yourself; you deserve more credit for your hard work.
The teacher never demeans students, always encouraging their efforts.
His habit of belittling his colleagues during meetings does nothing but demean him in the eyes of the entire team.

senses.definition: to express dissatisfaction or injustice about something

examples.x: Despite the improvements, employees continued to grouse about the working conditions.
Instead of enjoying the event, some attendees couldn't help but grouse about the organization and planning.
The students began to grouse when they learned about the increase in homework assignments.
During the meeting, team members started to grouse about the lack of communication from the leadership.
Despite the delicious meal, the customer began to grouse about the service at the restaurant.

senses.definition: to express mild disapproval, often in a gentle or corrective manner

examples.x: The teacher chided the student for talking loudly during the exam.
The mother chided her child for not wearing a coat on a chilly day.
The coach chided the team for their lack of teamwork during the crucial match.

senses.definition: to give a strong, negative review or opinion about something

examples.x: The movie critic panned the new film, citing poor acting and a weak storyline.
Despite high expectations, the restaurant was panned by customers for its slow service and bland food.
The product review in the magazine panned the latest gadget, highlighting its numerous technical flaws.
The book was panned by literary experts for its lack of originality and predictable plot.

senses.definition: to go against an argument or statement

examples.x: The evidence clearly contravenes the defendant's testimony.
Her alibi was contravened by video evidence placing her at the scene of the crime.
Test results contravened the manufacturer's claims about the product's efficacy.
